meta.title: Daily Reflection Tree
meta.description: An end-of-day reflection tool guiding employees through three psychological axes: Locus of Control, Contribution vs Entitlement, and Self-Centrism vs Altrocentrism.

nodes.text: Good evening. Before the day slips away, let's take a few minutes to look back — not to judge, just to notice.
If you had to describe today in a single word, what would it be?
options: Productive
Tough
Mixed
Frustrating

answers: Productive
Mixed
Tough
Frustrating

nodes.text: You said "{OPENING_Q.answer}." When something went well today, what do you think made it happen?
options: I prepared and it paid off
The right people showed up at the right time
I adjusted my approach mid-way
Honestly, things just fell into place

answers: I prepared and it paid off
I adjusted my approach mid-way
The right people showed up at the right time
Honestly, things just fell into place

nodes.text: So you see your hand in today's outcome. When things go this well, what do you usually do with that momentum?
options: I set myself up for tomorrow
I take a moment to appreciate the effort
I share what worked with someone else
I just move on to the next thing

nodes.text: Circumstances helped today. But think carefully — was there a choice you made, even a small one, that put you in position to benefit?
options: Yes — I positioned myself to be ready
Maybe — I showed up, which counts
Not really — it was mostly timing
I hadn't thought about it that way

answers: Yes — I positioned myself to be ready
Maybe — I showed up, which counts
Not really — it was mostly timing
I hadn't thought about it that way

nodes.text: You said "{OPENING_Q.answer}." When things got difficult today, what was your first instinct?
options: Figure out what part I could control
Wait and see if it resolved itself
Push through on my own
Feel stuck and frustrated

answers: Figure out what part I could control
Push through on my own
Wait and see if it resolved itself
Feel stuck and frustrated

nodes.text: Even on a hard day, you looked for a lever to pull. What kept you engaged instead of checking out?
options: I care about the outcome
I didn't want to let people down
It's just how I'm wired — I don't quit easily
I knew no one else would handle it

nodes.text: When you felt stuck, what would have made the biggest difference?
options: Someone acknowledging the difficulty
A clearer direction from leadership
More time or resources
Honestly, just knowing it wasn't all on me

answers: Someone acknowledging the difficulty
Honestly, just knowing it wasn't all on me
A clearer direction from leadership
More time or resources

nodes.text: You see your hand in what happened today — not because everything went perfectly, but because you stayed in the driver's seat. Agency isn't about controlling outcomes. It's about recognizing where you had a choice, and that you made one.

A tough day has a way of pulling your attention outward — to what others did or didn't do, to circumstances that felt unfair. That's human. But somewhere in today, even buried under the frustration, you made a call. You chose to push through, or to wait, or to endure. That's still agency — even if it doesn't feel like it yet.
You've been thinking about how you handled today. Now let's shift — from how you navigated things to what you gave.
Think about one interaction you had today. Which of these best captures it?
options: I helped someone with something that wasn't my job
I taught or explained something to a colleague
I felt I deserved more recognition than I got
I did my part — no more, no less

answers: I helped someone with something that wasn't my job
I taught or explained something to a colleague
I felt I deserved more recognition than I got
I did my part — no more, no less

nodes.text: You went beyond what was required. What motivated that?
options: It felt like the right thing to do
I wanted to be seen as reliable
The other person clearly needed it
I enjoy being useful — it gives me energy

answers: It felt like the right thing to do
The other person clearly needed it
I wanted to be seen as reliable
I enjoy being useful — it gives me energy

nodes.text: When you contribute without expecting anything back, how does the rest of your day usually feel?
options: Lighter — like I'm part of something
The same — I don't think about it much
Sometimes unappreciated, honestly
Good in the moment, but I wonder if it's sustainable

nodes.text: There's nothing wrong with wanting to be valued. But notice the distinction: did you give because you wanted to help, or because you wanted something in return?
options: Mostly to help — the recognition would just be nice
I'm not sure anymore, honestly
Both — and I think that's okay
Probably more for the recognition, if I'm honest

nodes.text: You mentioned feeling under-recognized or doing exactly your share. What's behind that today?
options: I've been giving a lot lately and it's not being seen
I'm protecting my energy — I've been burned before
I genuinely feel the balance is off
I'm just tired and didn't have more to give

answers: I've been giving a lot lately and it's not being seen
I genuinely feel the balance is off
I'm protecting my energy — I've been burned before
I'm just tired and didn't have more to give

nodes.text: Fair enough — recognition matters. But here's a question: if no one ever noticed your contributions, would you still make them?
options: Yes — I do it for the work itself
Probably, but it would wear on me over time
No — I need to know it matters to someone
I've never thought about it that way

nodes.text: Self-protection is wise when it's intentional. Is today's boundary a choice you're making — or a wall that went up on its own?
options: A conscious choice — I need to recharge
Somewhere in between
More of a reflex, if I'm honest
I'm not sure I can tell the difference anymore

nodes.text: You gave something today that wasn't required — and you didn't need anyone to see it. That kind of contribution is rare. It's not selflessness (you're not erasing yourself). It's the kind of generosity that comes from having something to give and choosing to give it. That's worth noticing.

Most real giving is mixed — some altruism, some desire to be seen, some genuine care. The honest answer is rarely pure. What matters is that you showed up for someone today. The motivation can be messy and the contribution can still be real.

Wanting recognition isn't a character flaw — it's a signal that you care about your work. The question is whether that need is running you or you're running it. When you can give freely and let recognition be a pleasant surprise rather than a requirement, the work itself starts to feel different.
You've reflected on what you gave today. Now one more lens — let's look at who was in your frame.
When you think about today's biggest challenge, who comes to mind first?
options: Just me — it was my problem to deal with
My team — we were all feeling it
A specific colleague who had it harder than me
The people we're ultimately doing this work for

answers: Just me — it was my problem to deal with
My team — we were all feeling it
A specific colleague who had it harder than me
The people we're ultimately doing this work for

nodes.text: When your frame is tightly focused on yourself, it often intensifies whatever you're feeling. Did you notice anyone else around you today who might have been going through something similar?
options: Yes, but I was too caught up in my own situation
Maybe — I wasn't really paying attention
Actually, yes — and I wish I'd reached out
No — my situation felt unique

answers: Yes, but I was too caught up in my own situation
Actually, yes — and I wish I'd reached out
Maybe — I wasn't really paying attention
No — my situation felt unique

nodes.text: You noticed someone else, even if you couldn't act on it. That noticing matters. If tomorrow brought a similar day, would you want to do anything differently?
options: I'd check in on that person
I'd try to widen my lens a bit more
I'd focus on my own situation first — I need to
I'm not sure yet

nodes.text: Your frame naturally included others today. What pulled your attention beyond yourself?
options: I could see someone struggling and it affected me
The work we're doing matters to people beyond our team
I realized my problem was smaller in context
Someone else's situation put mine in perspective

answers: I could see someone struggling and it affected me
Someone else's situation put mine in perspective
The work we're doing matters to people beyond our team
I realized my problem was smaller in context

nodes.text: You felt someone else's experience today — not just thought about it, but felt it. That capacity to take another's perspective is one of the most quietly powerful things a person can do at work. What did you do with that awareness?
options: I said something — offered support or acknowledgment
I adjusted my own behavior to make things easier for them
I held it quietly — I wasn't sure what to do
I carried it with me, and it changed how I saw my own day

nodes.text: Today your frame was centered on you — your stress, your work, your challenge. That's not wrong. But here's something worth sitting with: most emotional pain at work gets amplified by a tight frame. When all you see is your own situation, it fills the entire screen. Widening the lens — even slightly — doesn't minimize your experience. It contextualizes it. And context is where relief lives.

You were focused inward today, but you still noticed someone else. That's the seed of something — the moment you see beyond your own frame, even while you're hurting, you've already started to widen the lens. You don't have to act on it every time. Just keep noticing.

You held a wider frame today. Your challenges didn't disappear, but they found their place inside something larger — the team, the mission, the people you're ultimately serving. That wider frame is where meaning lives. Not because your problems don't matter, but because they matter alongside other things too.

You didn't just think about someone else today — you felt their experience. Perspective-taking like that isn't sympathy from a distance. It's the real thing: understanding that lands in your body, not just your head. That capacity — to hold your own day and someone else's at the same time — is what the best colleagues are made of.

Here's what today looked like through these three lenses:

🔹 **Agency:** You leaned toward ownership — seeing your hand in what happened, whether it went well or not.
🔹 **Contribution:** You oriented toward giving — showing up for others without keeping score.
🔹 **Radius:** Your frame extended beyond yourself — you held space for the team, a colleague, or the bigger mission.

That's a strong day of showing up. Not perfect — no day is. But intentional. Carry that into tomorrow.

Here's what today looked like through these three lenses:

🔹 **Agency:** You felt more acted upon than acting — the day happened to you more than you happened to it.
🔹 **Contribution:** Your attention was on what you needed or deserved — which is honest, even if it's uncomfortable.
🔹 **Radius:** Your frame was tight around yourself — your stress, your needs, your frustrations.

None of this is a verdict. It's a snapshot. The fact that you sat here and answered honestly? That already shifts something. Tomorrow is another data point. See what you notice.

That's your day, reflected. Sleep on it. Tomorrow is another chance to notice, choose, give, and widen the frame.

See you tomorrow evening.

axis1.internal: You leaned toward ownership today — seeing your choices, even in difficulty.
axis1.external: Today felt more like it happened to you — which is honest. The next step is finding where you still had a choice.

axis2.contribution: You oriented toward giving — your attention was on what you could offer, not what you were owed.
axis2.entitlement: Your attention was on what you needed or deserved. That's not wrong — but it's worth noticing where you might have given, too.

axis3.others: Your frame included others today — colleagues, the team, the people you serve.
axis3.self: Your frame was centered on yourself. Widening it even slightly might change how tomorrow feels.
